steps.title: Identify the paradox the answer must resolve
steps.content: Calculation Mode aims to minimize both financial cost and personal inconvenience. Yet participants using it chose the standard plan even when the green plan was cheaper. Since the financial cost argument doesn't explain the choice, something else must — the green plan's cap on usage (an inconvenience) must have tipped the balance.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'were equally unlikely to factor the financial savings of the green plan into their decision-making as were participants using either the Affect or Role Modes.': Calculation Mode explicitly involves weighing financial costs; this contradicts how the mode is defined. ✗
  • Choice B — 'may have been less strongly motivated to appear socially responsible with their choice of plan than they realized.': Social responsibility is a Role Mode concern, not a Calculation Mode concern. ✗
  • Choice C — 'may have determined that the green plan imposed additional burdens on them that were not sufficiently offset by the potential financial savings.': Fits exactly — the cap on usage is an inconvenience burden that outweighed the financial savings in a two-factor calculation. ✓
  • Choice D — 'were less likely to believe that the green plan was truly cost-effective than were participants using either the Affect or Role Modes.': The text does not say participants doubted the cost data; it says they still chose the pricier option despite knowing the green plan was cheaper. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
